title: Parametrically induced strong coupling between a superconducting quantum circuit and a solid-state spin ensemble
authors: Alejandro E. Baptista, Jinwoong Kim, Sonia Rani, Xi Cao, Wolfgang Pfaff
abstract: Efficient quantum state transfer between superconducting circuits and solid-state spins would unlock high-coherence quantum memories for superconducting quantum processors. We demonstrate dynamically controlled strong coupling between a Josephson circuit and a rare-earth spin ensemble. Using a parametric pump, we realize on-demand coupling of several MHz, which will enable faithful state transfer between quantum circuits and spins. Our architecture enables quantum control of spin ensembles, and paves the way for hybrid memories with coherence far beyond those of superconducting circuits alone.
summary: This paper demonstrates a novel architecture for creating high-coherence quantum memories by coupling a superconducting quantum circuit to a solid-state spin ensemble. By using a parametric pump, the researchers induced dynamic strong coupling, enabling fast and controllable quantum state transfer between the two disparate quantum systems.
main_problem: The primary goal is to achieve efficient, on-demand, and tunable quantum state transfer between superconducting quantum circuits and solid-state spin ensembles to build high-coherence quantum memories.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated dynamic strong coupling using a parametric pump, enabling fast quantum state transfer between the superconducting circuit and the spin ensemble.
method: The research employed microwave spectroscopy, measuring transmission and reflection, and analyzing avoided crossings induced by a parametric pump tone.
model_or_system: The system is a hybrid device coupling a superconducting quantum circuit (SNAIL mixer) to a microwave cavity, which is dispersively coupled to a rare-earth spin ensemble ($	ext{}^{171}	ext{Yb}^{3+}:	ext{YSO}$). The dynamics are modeled by coupled Hamiltonian terms involving the circuit, cavity, and spin operators.
key_observables: Avoided crossings in spectroscopy, measured coupling strengths ($	ilde{g}_{mc}$, $	ilde{g}_{ms}$), and the resulting state transfer efficiency.
important_parameters: Operating temperature ($10 	ext{ mK}$), static couplings ($\approx 27 	ext{ MHz}$), and the pump amplitude ($\xi$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the dispersive regime approximation and the use of the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) for modeling the coupled dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup (Josephson element $ightarrow$ cavity $ightarrow$ spin ensemble), mode characterization via spectroscopy, and the measurement scheme for pump-activated interactions showing avoided crossings.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing the static couplings in the dispersive regime to demonstrating the dynamic strong coupling mechanism using a parametric pump, culminating in the prediction of fast state transfer.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the hardware challenge of quantum memory integration by coupling superconducting circuits (a major QI platform) to long-coherence solid-state spins, which is a key area in quantum networking and computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experiment design. (a) Our goal is on-demand, tunable strong coupling via a mixer (purple), ˆm. (b) Lumped element circuit to illustrate our tunable coupling experiment. (c) 3D cross-section sketch of the cavity used to implement the architecture. The cavity (yellow), ˆc, hosts a chip containing a SNAIL coupler (purple), ˆm, spin ensemble crystal (green), ˆs, and dielectric slab (light yellow) for cavity frequency tun- ing. Red arrows show the oscillating fields for the differential cavity mode that couple capacitively to the SNAIL and induc- tively to the spins. A flux coil is inserted near the SNAIL loop for magnetic flux tuning. (d) Spin system: 171Yb3+:YSO; cav- ity frequency is...

FIG. 2. Mode characterization. (a) Measurement scheme: The cavity was probed as a function of the reduced external flux (φext = Φext/Φ0) applied to the SNAIL. Mode hybridization leads to a modest flux tuning of the cavity mode. System is probed by measuring transmission and re- flection of the cavity. (b) Cavity spectroscopy vs. φext. Gray bar indicates cavity-SNAIL coupling. The arrow labeled fs is at the spin transition frequency. (c) Line cut at φext = 0.237 (pink marker in (b)). (d) Line cut at φext = 0.5 (blue marker in (b)). (e) Polariton ring-up and ring-down at cavity-spin resonance (gray dashed line in (c)). The cavity was driven at fc with a 7-µs pulse, and response was recorded...

FIG. 3. Parametrically induced strong coupling. (a) At fixed φext the SNAIL is driven near SNAIL-cavity (ma- genta) or SNAIL-spin (blue) difference frequency. (b) Cavity spectroscopy in the presence of the pump. Dashed lines are simulated frequencies of the 3 coupled modes in the system when the drive is on. Here ∆pump/2π = fpump −∆mc/2π is the difference between the pump frequency, fpump, and the cavity-SNAIL detuning, ∆mc/2π. (c) Line cut of the SNAIL-cavity avoided crossing (pink marker in (b)). (d) Line cut of the SNAIL-spin avoided crossing (blue marker in (b)). (e) SNAIL-cavity coupling, ˜gmc, extracted from normal mode splittings vs. pump strength, ξ, and external flux. (f) Observed...

FIG. 4. Suppression of observed parametric spin cou- pling. (a) Strong pump-induced coupling between SNAIL and spins (solid blue) also induces a detuned coupling to the nearby cavity (dashed light blue). (b) True parametric cou- pling, ˜gms, inferred from the observed coupling, ˜g∗ ms. (c) Overlap of each driven eigenmode of the system (as shown spectroscopically in Fig. 3(b)) with the bare SNAIL, cavity, and spin modes, from left to right.

FIG. 5. DisCoAlPaCa Design and Simulation. (a) Dis- CoAlPaCa assembly with caps and an American quarter for scale (24.257 mm diameter); colored outlines indicate mode elements following the paper’s color scheme. The tuning di- electric is held in the right cavity gap by a clamp. The flux coil is held in a copper cap over the SNAIL chip. Its position is indicated with a black dashed line. (b) Simulated electric and magnetic fields of the differential mode; magenta arrows indicate field direction.
